schools.summary: The huge polished stones and Herod's unfinished temple (Josephus, John 2:20); Jesus' final, tragic departure after Matthew 23.
Raban/Remigius: literal overthrow under Titus, the temple removed lest believers relapse to Judaism; Jerome allegorises the fall of the Law.
Calvin: the disciples dazzled by the splendour, unable to believe Christ's kingdom could rise; like those who admire Popery's pomp.
Origen: each man is God's temple, ruined when sin drives Christ away.
Henry: the house Christ leaves is desolate indeed; foresight of fading glory weans us from it.
Literal fulfilment in A.D. 70 (Josephus): the temple burnt against Titus's will, the foundations ploughed up (Maimonides; Micah 3:12).
Calvin: the temple's wealth veiled the disciples' eyes; Christ swears its ruin, God's vengeance on the nation that rejected his Son.
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry expounds the name 'Jesus Christ' and Joshua typology, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the name Jesus Christ, not on the verse.

schools.summary: Three questions answered by intermingling Jerusalem's fall and the end; first use of 'parousia'; Robertson's painter image of blended perspectives; Clarke's 'end of the age' = Jewish economy.
Calvin: the disciples, bred to think the temple eternal, confound the commencement of Christ's reign with its consummation, grasping triumph before warfare.
Chrysostom, Jerome, Hilary count three questions separated by times; Origen/Remigius allegorise the Mount of Olives as the Gentile Church.
Cyril of Jerusalem reads the question as a safeguard against being deceived by Antichrist.

debates.question: Do the three questions refer to one event or several?

sides.position: Distinct events (Jerusalem / coming / end) blended in one answer
The disciples confusedly merged them, expecting all at once

schools.summary: The first sign is false Christs amid universal messianic expectation; the verb 'lead astray' runs through the discourse; Peter the likely source (Ellicott).
Henry's double-reference key: the prophecy primarily concerns Jerusalem but, as a type, looks forward to the end of the world.
Calvin: the disciples must finish the course; the gospel is seed sown — wait patiently; beware false teachers and be not terrified by scandals.
Wesley: the caution is chiefly for succeeding Christians; many things reach beyond A.D. 70.
Cyril of Jerusalem applies the warning to the final Antichrist.
False Messiahs documented from Josephus (Simon Magus, Theudas, the Egyptian, Acts 21:38); Ellicott cautions no named Messianic pretender before A.D. 70.
Calvin: God permitted impostors to delude the Jews who rejected his Son; believers must not faint at the multitude carried away.
Wesley: never so many impostors as just before the fall, when the Messiah was generally expected.
Cyril of Jerusalem: fulfilled in part in Simon and Menander, and to recur.
The wars and commotions before A.D. 70 (four emperors, Caligula, Claudius); 'be not troubled' (2 Thessalonians 2:2) against date-setting.
Augustine's threefold key (Jerusalem/Church/judgment); Chrysostom on providential wrath; Jerome reads the strife of heretics; Raban: the warning kept the apostles from fleeing too soon.
Calvin: nothing happens without God's providence; prepare for endurance, not ease.
Wesley distinguishes near 'wars' from distant 'rumours'; the end is far from these beginnings.
Cyril of Jerusalem points to the Persian-Roman wars of his day.
Wars (Caesarea, Alexandria, Otho/Vitellius), famine (Agabus, Acts 11:28; Claudius), pestilence (text-critically doubtful), earthquakes — all from Josephus and Tacitus; Lightfoot's vivid picture.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): are there not wars, famines, earthquakes now? Watch therefore.
'Beginning of sorrows' = birth-pangs, the Jewish 'sorrows of the Messiah'; heavier woes to come; the travail issues in a new birth (Matthew 19:28; Romans 8:22).
Calvin: not to consume themselves with grief but to reckon on a long exercise of patience.
Persecution fulfilled through Acts (Peter, Stephen, James, Paul, Nero); the despised Name now glorious; Tacitus on Christians hated.
Origen/Remigius refer it to the end — persecution everywhere when idols are deserted; the Augustine/Gloss debate on whether the gospel was preached 'in all the world' before A.D. 70 (fame vs fruit).
Chrysostom adds the third source of opposition — false brethren (2 Corinthians 11:26).
Calvin: the disciples bruised like wheat under the millstone, hated and punished as criminals, yet for the gospel's sake.

debates.question: Was the gospel 'preached in all the world' fulfilled before A.D. 70?

sides.position: Not yet wholly fulfilled (fruit in every nation)
Fulfilled as to the fame of preaching before the fall

schools.summary: Apostates betray others for safety (1 John 2:19); Tacitus on the Neronian betrayals; the Zealot faction-fights (Ellicott).
Calvin: a violent temptation — Christ a stone of stumbling (1 Peter 2:8); Gill: fair-weather professors stumble at the cross.
Wesley: against false prophets hold faith, against iniquity love, against all hope to the end.
Cyril of Jerusalem applies it to bishops against bishops in his own day.
False prophets during the siege (Josephus) and the heretical teachers of the apostolic age (2 Timothy 2:17; 2 Peter 2:1; 1 Timothy 4:1); warned already in Matthew 7:15.
Calvin distinguishes these from v.5 — false teachers will arise in all ages, crafty to draw away sects; the tempest of error tries our faith.
'Iniquity'/'lawlessness' chills Christian ardour; forsaking the assembly (Hebrews 10:25); love giving way to hatred and suspicion; the apostasy of 2 Thessalonians 2:3.
Calvin: even good minds lose the desire to do good amid wickedness; the remedy is not to faint in well-doing (2 Thessalonians 3:13).
Cyril of Jerusalem applies it to feigned friendships in his own day.
'End' as either the fall of Jerusalem (deliverance, Luke 21:18-19) or final salvation at life's close; Ellicott: both a higher and lower sense.
Clarke: not one Christian perished in the fall — they escaped when Cestius Gallus raised the siege; perseverance saves.

debates.question: Does 'endure unto the end' mean the end of the war or the end of life?

sides.position: The end of the Jewish war — deliverance from the fall
The end of life — final salvation

schools.summary: The gospel spread through the empire before A.D. 70 (Colossians 1:23; Romans 1:8); 'all the world' = the Roman oikoumene (Luke 2:1); 'a witness to all nations' sanctions the Gentile mission.
Calvin: not literally every portion of the world, but the gospel reaching the farthest bounds before the last coming.
Wesley: preached in general before the fall; Josephus the best commentary on the chapter.
Cyril of Jerusalem: nearly the whole world now filled with Christ's doctrine.

debates.question: What is the scope of 'all the world' and which 'end'?

sides.position: The Roman empire, and the end = the fall of Jerusalem
The world's farthest bounds, before the last coming

schools.summary: The Roman army with its idolatrous ensigns (Luke 21:20; Daniel 9:27); Ellicott prefers the Zealots' profanation of the temple; 'let the reader understand' is Mark's insertion.
Jerome: a statue of Caesar/Hadrian or Antichrist; Hilary: Antichrist claiming God's honour (2 Thessalonians 2:4); Origen/Pseudo-Augustine allegorise 'false word'.
Calvin: the profanation and end of the temple, confirmed from Daniel.
Chrysostom/Pseudo-Chrysostom: the desolating army; Cyril of Jerusalem: the Antichrist of 2 Thessalonians 2:4.

debates.question: What is the abomination of desolation?

sides.position: The Roman army/ensigns (Luke 21:20)
The Zealots' profanation of the temple
Antichrist in the holy place
A statue of Caesar or Hadrian

outliers.note: Allegorises the abomination as 'false word' standing in the Scriptures.

schools.summary: The Christians fled to Pella in A.D. 68 (Eusebius); the providential withdrawal of Cestius Gallus gave the opening; the mountains of Gilead as refuge.
Chrysostom: when the army appears there is no hope of safety; unlike Sennacherib or the Maccabees, no recovery now; Remigius on the flight to Pella.
Calvin: the calamity so sudden flight will scarcely be possible; the design to wean from ease and fortify.
Wesley: the impolitic retreat of Cestius Gallus was the signal.
Flat roofs reached by outside stairs, forming continuous terraces to the gate; instant flight without stopping for goods (Jeremiah 45:5); the danger from the factions within.
The peasant left his cloak at home; he must flee the field at once, for once the army encircles the city escape is impossible; the Christians' obedience preserved them (Eusebius).
The 'woe' is pity not curse — expectant and nursing mothers could not flee and bore the worst of the siege (Luke 23:28-29); the famine horrors recorded by Josephus.
Winter would make flight a double calamity (Lightfoot's Rabbi Tanchum); the sabbath would limit them to a mile and find gates shut (Exodus 16:29); pray for mitigation though the doom is certain.
Wesley: they prayed, and their flight was in the spring.
Unparalleled tribulation (Daniel 12:1) verified by Josephus — slaughter, captivity (Luke 21:24), the days of vengeance; nothing parallel to Israel's breaking-up (Ellicott).
Calvin: the tribulation answers the nation's guilt in wreaking cruelty on the Physician himself.
Wesley raises a farther futurist meaning yet to be fulfilled.
Cyril of Jerusalem refers it to the tribulation under Antichrist, confined to a few days.

debates.question: Is the 'great tribulation' wholly fulfilled in A.D. 70 or also future?

sides.position: Fulfilled in the fall of Jerusalem (Josephus)
Also a greater future tribulation under Antichrist

schools.summary: The war would have extirpated the nation (Josephus: 1.1 million perished); shortened by the factions and Titus's swift success; the elect are the Christians (Romans 8:33).
Calvin: but for God's sparing they would be as Sodom (Isaiah 1:9); a remnant shall be saved (Romans 9:29); Lightfoot on lengthening then shortening for the elect.
Cyril of Jerusalem refers the shortening to Antichrist's three-and-a-half years (Daniel 7:25).
'Then' leaps to the consummation; Christ's coming will be open, not hidden; Augustine: the wicked work miracles yet are not higher with God; the elect cannot truly be deceived (Gregory, Raban).
In the city's extremity the Jews caught at any impostor (Deuteronomy 13:1-3); false Christs offer a way out of the troubles.
Calvin: in desperate straits men are deceived under God's name, by false christs armed with confounding signs.
Cyril of Jerusalem: hatred of brethren is the prelude to Antichrist (2 Thessalonians 2:3).
Bar-Cochba and Josephus's sorcerers; Simon Magus and Elymas; 'signs and lying wonders' (2 Thessalonians 2:9); signs do not prove God's power; 'if possible' implies it is not possible.
Calvin: added to excite vigilance, yet the elect are safe — cannot fall away, kept by the Son of God (John 10:28); Wesley: God will not suffer the body of Christians to be deceived.
The forewarning leaves them inexcusable and arms them; the fulfilment proves Christ's omniscience and Messiahship (Mark 13:23, 'take heed').
Calvin: those dismayed by predicted stumbling-blocks are inexcusable; God is faithful and gives strength to resist (1 Corinthians 10:13).
Impostors led people into the desert (the Egyptian, Acts 21:38) or claimed Christ in secret chambers (Simon son of Gioras, John of Giscala); they shun publicity that would test them.
Calvin contrasts concealment with the sudden far-reaching extension of Christ's kingdom; walk in the light while you have it (John 12:35).
The simile is of manner, not direction — a sudden, public, unmistakable coming; the same words cover both Jerusalem's fall and the final Advent (Barnes).
Clarke: the lightning 'out of the east' traces the Roman army's westward march; Wesley: as quick as lightning, no time for warning.

debates.question: Does 'the coming of the Son of man' here mean the fall of Jerusalem or the final Advent?

sides.position: Primarily Jerusalem's destruction (Christ's coming in judgment)
The final Advent, or both by double reference

schools.summary: The carcass = doomed Jerusalem, the eagles = the Roman legions (whose ensigns bore the eagle); a proverb of judgment converging on the spiritually dead (Job 39:30).
Calvin: argues from the less to the greater — believers should flock to Christ the Author of life as birds to a carcass; rejects allegorizing the eagles.

debates.question: What are the carcass and the eagles?

sides.position: Dead Jerusalem and the Roman legions / general judgment
Christ and the believers gathering to him

schools.summary: Apocalyptic idiom for the fall of a power (Isaiah 13:10; Ezekiel 32:7); preterists (Lightfoot, Gill, Clarke) apply it to Jerusalem by 'immediately'; others to the Advent's wider range.
The last day: tribulation of Antichrist, the sign of the cross brighter than the sun (Chrysostom); lights dimmed not destroyed (Jerome); the Church under persecution (Augustine).
Calvin: a general recapitulation of all the Church's afflictions; Christ's glory delayed till the whole course of redemption is complete.

debates.question: Does 'immediately after the tribulation' refer to A.D. 70 or the final Advent?

sides.position: The destruction of Jerusalem, in symbolic language
The second Advent / the whole course of the Church's afflictions

schools.summary: The 'sign' is the Son of Man himself coming in the clouds (Daniel 7:13), not a cross in the sky (Ellicott, Robertson); the tribes mourn (Zechariah 12:10; Revelation 1:7).
Origen (with Cyril): the sign is the luminous Cross going before the King, that the Jews may mourn.
Calvin: the hidden majesty of Christ's kingdom is then openly displayed as a standard compelling the world to look; Henry: a sign once spoken against, now admired.

debates.question: What is 'the sign of the Son of man'?

sides.position: Christ himself coming in glory (apposition)
The luminous Cross in heaven
The visible vengeance on Jerusalem

themes: sign of the Son of man

schools.summary: Preterists (Gill, Lightfoot, Clarke): 'angels' = gospel ministers gathering the Gentile elect after Jerusalem's fall; futurists (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson): the angelic harvest at the resurrection (1 Thessalonians 4:16).
Calvin: pastoral comfort — the scattered Church will be gathered not by human means but by heavenly power.
Chrysostom: the trumpet is the resurrection; the angels gather the risen to the clouds.

debates.question: Who are the 'angels' and the gathering?

sides.position: Gospel ministers gathering the Gentile elect after A.D. 70
Literal angels gathering the elect at the resurrection

schools.summary: Henry: Grotius's key distinction — 'these things' (v.34) befall this generation, but 'that day and hour' (v.36) is unknown; don't confound them (2 Thessalonians 2:2).
The budding fig (likely seen on Olivet) heralds nearness — of Jerusalem's ruin (Barnes, Clarke) or of the Advent (Ellicott).
Calvin: as spring softens the tree to make way for new growth, the Church's afflictions precede its immortal glory (2 Corinthians 4:16).
Allegory: the fig tree as the human race (Augustine) or the Synagogue with Antichrist its branch (Hilary); winter-to-summer of the Spirit (Origen).
Applies the fig parable: the signs herald nearness; Luke's 'your redemption draweth nigh' (Luke 21:28); better reading 'He is near' (James 5:9); the four apostles as representatives of all believers.
Chrysostom: as the fig's budding necessarily brings summer, so the foretold event follows in quick succession — calm for the righteous, winter for sinners.
'This generation' = the contemporaries then living; Jerusalem fell ~40 years later (some apostles, esp. John, survived); Ellicott: no warrant for 'race.'
Calvin: within one generation the city and temple were destroyed; future ages are not exempt, but the prediction was for that age.
Chrysostom: 'this generation' = the generation of the faithful, which no trial shall overthrow.

sides.position: The contemporaries then living (fulfilled in A.D. 70)
The Jewish race, enduring to the end
The generation of the faithful

schools.summary: Christ claims for his own words the eternity of Jehovah's (Psalms 102:26; Isaiah 40:8; 1 Peter 1:24-25); verified by the abiding pre-eminence of his words in history.
Calvin: raises the mind above the world's billowing flux — our salvation rests unshaken on Christ's promises, not on the changeful world.
Against the Arians: 'the Son knows not' = makes not known (Augustine, Jerome, Origen); the clause is absent from the best Greek copies.
Calvin: the two natures each retain their properties — Christ as man did not know the day, no more derogating from his deity than his mortality did.
The day is deliberately hidden (Robertson: of the second coming, not v.34's events); Ellicott: a real self-emptying limitation of the incarnate Word (Philippians 2:7).

debates.question: In what sense does 'the Son' not know the day?

sides.position: He knows but does not reveal it ('makes not known')
A real limitation of his human nature through the kenosis

schools.summary: Noah's flood types a sudden judgment on a secure, feasting world (the Petrine echo, 1 Peter 3:20; 2 Peter 3:6); ample warning, utter unpreparedness.
Calvin: the sin is not eating/drinking but stupid indifference and obstinacy — living as if there were no Judge.
Chrysostom: a sudden, unlooked-for coming amid 'Peace and safety' (1 Thessalonians 5:3).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to another discourse, not this verse.

schools.summary: The lawful round of life (eating, marrying) is not itself sin; the fault is being so engrossed in the present as to be heedless of coming judgment.
The willful ignorance of Noah's age types the careless world; Ellicott: the world's nescience proves the Advent's signs are spiritual, discerned only by faith.
Calvin: their ignorance was unbelief; Noah by faith saw the hidden vengeance (Hebrews 11:7) — direct your mind to the small saved company (1 Peter 3:20).
A sudden, character-based separation of those outwardly alike; Ellicott (from Greek usage) and Wesley read 'taken' as saved, Barnes leans to 'taken by death.'
Calvin: remember Lot's wife (Genesis 19:26); let no earthly tie retard the godly — out of one couple, one may be taken and one left.
Cyril: the Judge is no respecter of persons — the angels will take even the husbandman, the slave, the poor.

debates.question: Is the one 'taken' saved or destroyed?

sides.position: Taken = received to Christ (saved)
Taken = taken away by death/judgment

schools.summary: The hand-mill worked by two women (Exodus 11:5); a companion image to v.40 (perhaps seen on Olivet), teaching the same sudden, inward separation.
Cyril: comfort to the lowliest — even the woman compelled to grind is not despised; the Judge is no respecter of persons.
The hour is hidden to keep us in perpetual expectation (Jerome); watching = casting off sloth (Gregory); it applies to every believer and every death (Augustine).
Calvin: the uncertainty, far from excusing sloth, is the express spur to unrelaxing watchfulness; Henry: keep the temper you'd wish the Lord to find.
The watchful escaped A.D. 70, the careless perished (Clarke); reinforced by a series of parables (Robertson).
The thief-parable argues from common prudence to vigilance; the day comes 'as a thief in the night' (1 Thessalonians 5:2); these verses parallel Luke 12:42 ff.
Calvin: as fear of robbers banishes sleep, so the sudden coming forbids indolence — and what is at stake is not property but the soul.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to the parable of the two sons, not this verse.

schools.summary: Be ready, for the hour is unknown; the signs rouse watchfulness, not date-setting (Ellicott); 'it is useless to set the day, folly to neglect it' (Robertson).
Calvin: the delayed judgment hangs over us every hour — no excuse for indolence, with the soul at stake.
A special charge to the rulers/bishops; faithful in not robbing the needy, prudent in giving each his due food (Origen); rare to find both united.
The minister feeds the flock 'meat in due season' (John 21:15-17); 'wise' = moral discernment, an art of spiritual dietetics (Ellicott); six ministerial duties (Clarke).
Calvin: a fortiori — if the people must watch, much more the apostles; the higher the rank, the heavier the burden of fidelity and wisdom.
Blessed is the servant found at his work; the blessing is the master's approbation, applying to the Advent, to death, and to the Lord's comings in life's crises (Ellicott).
Fidelity rewarded with enlarged trust; Barnes keeps it from meaning higher ecclesiastical rank; Ellicott sees continued joyful service in heaven (Luke 19:17).
Clarke: the faithful minister receives power to dispense covenant blessings with the Spirit's demonstration; unfaithfulness forfeits usefulness.
The secret thought 'my lord delayeth' is the scoffers' temper (2 Peter 3:3-4); forgetting that the Judge is ever near (James 5:9) breeds tyranny and sensuality (Ellicott).
Calvin: presuming on delay involves the servant in darkness and supposed impunity; the remedy is constant thought of the sudden coming.
The evil servant's secret unbelief issues in tyranny over fellow-servants and sensual indulgence — the unfaithful minister who beats the flock and feasts with the worldly (Barnes).
Gill: spiritual oppression — usurping dominion over consciences, imposing unbidden rites, persecuting the nonconforming saints.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here concerns the persecution of the prophets (Jeremiah 26; 2 Chronicles 24), not directly this verse.

schools.summary: The lord comes suddenly on the careless servant (1 Thessalonians 5:3); Clarke: a sudden death answering to his infidelity — his presumption of delay leaves him unprepared.
'Cut asunder' = an ancient execution (Daniel 2:5; Hebrews 11:37), or figuratively severing him from the saints to the hypocrites' portion; the double-minded man unmasked (Ellicott).
Henry: death and damnation — body to the grave, soul to hell; he who divided himself between Christ and the world is justly divided.
Clarke: the three punishments answer the three sins; the chapter's prophecy was minutely fulfilled in Jerusalem's fall, a pledge that grace's promises will be kept too.
